questions.question: What has he ___ (mean) by that comment?
She has ___ (become) a very successful lawyer.
The sun has ___ (shine) all week.
Have you ___ (spell) your name correctly?
She has ___ (weep) all night.
He has ___ (kneel) before the queen.
I've ___ (spill) coffee all over my shirt!
She has ___ (hang) her coat by the door.
Has she ever ___ (beat) you at chess?
He has ___ (swear) to tell the truth.
They have ___ (forbid) entry to the building.
She has never ___ (forgive) him for lying.
Lightning has ___ (strike) the same spot twice.
My favourite jumper has ___ (shrink) in the wash.
They've ___ (bind) the pages together.
The workers have ___ (dig) a very deep hole.
He has ___ (grind) the coffee beans already.
He has ___ (spring) a surprise on everyone.
Have they ___ (stick) to the original plan?
She has ___ (withdraw) all her savings.

theory.explanation:
Irregular Past Participle forms – Part V (advanced)

Less common but important forms:
  mean   → meant      become → become     shine  → shone
  spell  → spelt      weep   → wept       kneel  → knelt
  spill  → spilt      hang   → hung        beat   → beaten
  swear  → sworn      forbid → forbidden  forgive → forgiven
  strike → struck     shrink → shrunk     bind   → bound
  dig    → dug        grind  → ground     spring → sprung
  stick  → stuck      withdraw → withdrawn

Note: become → became → become (past participle = infinitive)
Note: beat → beat/beaten (two acceptable pp forms)

examples.sentence: She has become a very successful lawyer.
examples.explanation: become → became → become. Past participle = infinitive! (not 'became' as pp)

examples.sentence: They have forbidden entry to the building.
examples.explanation: forbid → forbade → forbidden. The -en ending, like hidden, ridden.

examples.sentence: The workers have dug a very deep hole.
examples.explanation: dig → dug → dug. Past simple = past participle = dug (not 'digged').
